titleEn: O Namesake of the Prophet Who Prays

verses.ajuz: ربه مخلصا له في قل اوحي

translationEn: O namesake of the one who implores his Lord with sincerity in the revelation of 'Say, it has been revealed to me' — and the likeness of the one whom the whale carried from the well, submissive as a weary camel — and one whose honorific name is that of the noble Messenger, after the Messiah: speak plainly today, O gazing, lovelorn man, in speech that comes from the depths of a wounded heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to implore, to beseech earnestly
with sincerity, devoted
carried, bore (here: the whale bearing Jonah)
a weary, exhausted camel
yearns, longs ardently
one distraught with love, bewildered
wounded, lacerated (heart)

context: A short panegyric in which Abu Tammam addresses a patron who shares the name of the Prophet Muhammad, drawing on Quranic allusions — the supplication of Jonah and the revelation to the Prophet — to honor his namesake.

titleAr: يقول في قومس صحبي وقد أخذت
titleEn: My Companions Ask in Qumas

verses.sadr: يقول في قومس صحبي وقد أخذت
أمطلع الشمس تنوي أن تؤم بنا

translationEn: My companions say, in Qumas, as the night journey has seized us and the noble mares stride onward: 'Do you intend to lead us toward the place where the sun rises?' I said: No — rather, toward the place where generosity rises.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Qumas, a region in northeastern Iran (Khorasan)
night journey, traveling by night
thoroughbred mares of the Mahra breed
tall, long-necked noble camels or horses
to head toward, to aim for
the rising-place of generosity (i.e., the patron)

context: A two-verse gem in which Abu Tammam plays on the word 'mطلع' (rising-place), contrasting the sunrise in the east with the patron's generosity, suggesting he travels not toward the literal east but toward the source of bounty.

titleEn: May Joy Be Yours, Noble Scion

verses.sadr: جنى لي فيك من ثمرات مدحي

translationEn: May joy be yours, O noble scion — for you have gladdened me with a wholesome recovery, most delightful. May your life be long, your eyes contented, and may the assault of death be turned away from you. I see hopes smiling with bright teeth, beaming at your magnificent gifts. The light of the sun, when it rises, boasts of the radiance of your own splendid rising. You have built a structure of glory that soars high, and soared with the height of your glory in that edifice. You grew rich through the giving of your wealth in the pursuit of excellence, so your soul itself is enriched by its own bestowal. The tongue of gratitude has harvested for me, through praising you, choice verses among the fruits of my poetry. I have offered them to you, and in my estimation they are, across all time, among the most pure of gifts.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: noble scion, offspring of a great lineage
you were granted health/recovery
the onslaught of death, the lethal charge
smiling with white teeth (a description of hopes)
to boast, to vie in pride
the edifice, the built structure
freshly harvested, choice (verses)

context: A congratulatory panegyric celebrating a patron's recovery from illness. Abu Tammam interweaves themes of physical restoration, moral greatness, and poetic gratitude in a poem heavy with wordplay, particularly on the root ب-ن-ي (build/edifice) and the imagery of light.

titleEn: Your Tears Have Given Their Utmost
translationEn: Your tears have given their utmost effort, and so your heart has complained of its passionate longing. You have laden your soul with more than it can bear in love, and thus it has been broken. O you who gloat over me when you see the beloved's estrangement and rebuff — do not gloat, for he is a master who torments his servant.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its utmost effort, its maximum
its intense love, its passionate longing
it broke him, it wore him down
one who gloats over another's misfortune
turning away, rebuffing, estrangement
master, lord

context: A brief ghazal in the mجزوء الكامل meter, lamenting the pain of unrequited love and rebuking the gloater. The final verse turns on the paradox that a beloved who torments is still a master worthy of devotion.

titleEn: Woe to the Grieved from the Carefree

verses.sadr: فكم لي من هواء فيك صاف
وهل من جاء بعد الفتح يسعى

translationEn: Woe to the grieved man from the carefree, and to the worn-out encampment from one of those afflictions — for none visits the dwelling-place save one generous with his tears and lavish with his ribs. His weeping has flowed so long over the ruins that it has drawn dry their buckets like an exhausted well. May al-Sharatan and the Pleiades water your valley-bend and your earth with a drenching, lush, satisfying rain. How much pure air of yours I have breathed, nourishing its sky with longing and illness! A morning-fresh beauty arose, stretching tall in her trailing mantle over a supple coat of mail. She complains of weariness from one swift half when she rises and from one slow half. She taunts you with an eye bedewed — yet its ultimate effect is on a pure heart. I shall be grateful for the relief of the relaxed chest-band and the gentleness with which I outwit the stubborn age. For indeed I have for al-Hasan ibn Wahb a gift like a sudden downpour of rain. I say to the stumbling step of literary culture that has found shelter in his spacious warmth: 'Turn the white camels, breathing in their open spaces, toward the moon of the drinking-companions and the assembly!' For God has granted you a tongue that is elevated in its mention by the name of Abu Ali. Radiant he is — when one rolls in his bounty, one rolls in nobility and generosity of nature. By the life of the sons of Abi Dina, and by my own life, and by the life of my father and the sons of Adi — your letter has dispelled every hidden grief and struck the true mark in its aim. I broke its seal and its wonders shone forth for me from the manifest news. It was more delicate in my eyes, more soothing to my liver, than freshly gathered flowers. Its effect on me was more pleasant and welcome than good tidings arriving after an announcement of death. Its contents concealed what even the chests of singing-women concealed of jewels. How many a weighty dwelling of meaning is within it, and how many a beautiful expression! How much great goodness it has made plain, fulfilling a magnificent pledge! You wrote it without an unpleasant word, upon the ear without a foul pen-stroke. So release me from my hobbled hopes and from the shackles of rhymes and riding-beasts. In the scorching heat of Ramadan, it boils the head of one who is neither reserved nor pious. O cooling of the heart — it was like a burning stone — and O satiation of mine when it passes, by my Lord! A letter from one who has enjoyed life after a time and has given us pleasure from the best of culture. Though it traveled the earth as a first-born, it was presented to a worthy ear. If it be among your choice gifts, many a gift of yours is like a sacrifice. A clarity you did not inherit as a claim, nor did you draw it from a dried well. You walked in it against your enemies until you strode upon a fulfilled hope. Come, take me in journeys facing a direction — thoroughbreds slender as bent bows. For you will find none less in passion and spirit, nor more bound to closeness with the base. I grew up on white traits from you as jewelry grows on a loyal friend. From a generosity whose flow reached me upon rain and from a generosity that arrived — and from a generosity whose grinding sound surrounds me with its teeth, and from a young man's noble character. He of limited means is grieved by what trickles to me of this fortunate connection. He creeps toward me in a diminutive form, and peers from the edge of a hidden glance. He follows the blessing you give me with an eye of rancor, as an orphan looks at his guardian. Hoping that he might strike a spark to you by my flint and cut my very sinew — and that hope is his when the phoenix becomes domesticated and a eunuch's son grows old. I see that my brothers, when I was absent from them in the distant valley, had their loyalty returned to them like a marriage contract voided without a guardian. While you remained as their guiding star and they sailed by your wind morning and evening — then at that moment the bowman was left alone with his bow, and the equipment was poured upon the warrior. And they have indeed shown kindness, but the valley flooded and overwhelmed the villages. For does one who came after the conquest walk like a companion of two migrations alongside the Prophet?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the grieved, the sorrowful one
the carefree, the one free from worry
ruins, traces of abandoned encampments
a sudden, intense shower of rain
the phoenix, a mythical bird (used for impossibility)
the two migrations (to Abyssinia and Medina)
stones heated in fire, burning hot stones

context: A long qasida addressed to al-Hasan ibn Wahb, a prominent Abbasid secretary and literary figure, blending a nasib opening with effusive praise of the patron's letter and generosity. Abu Tammam celebrates the letter as if it were itself a gift surpassing all riches, and closes with reflections on jealousy and true loyalty.

titleEn: By the Rose Upon His Cheek
translationEn: No — by the rose upon his cheek and the grace in his stature — I have loved no one but him, even though he has afflicted me with his estrangement. If love has made me ill after the healing of his affection, perhaps after its fullness he will take pity on his servant.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rose; also rosy color on the cheek
his stature, his figure
has worn me thin, has whittled me down (with his rebuff)
his turning away, his rebuffing
healing, making healthy
to take pity on, to feel compassion for

context: A delicate four-verse ghazal in the mجزوء الخفيف meter, invoking the beloved's physical beauty as a kind of oath, and ending with the lover's hope that the beloved will show mercy after having caused suffering.

titleEn: A Caller Summoned with a Guide's Tongue

verses.ajuz: في قلب ذي سمر بها متهجد

verses.sadr: مالي إذا ما رضت فيك غربية
ما ذاك إلا أن زندك لم يكن
ولجأت منك إلى ابن ملك أنبأت

verses.ajuz: لا خير في شرف إذا لم أحمد
وأذب عن شرفي بما ملكت يدي

verses.sadr: كم من ضريك قد بسطت يمينه
لو أن هرثمة بن أعين في الورى
وطلعت في درج العلى حتى إذا

verses.ajuz: من جمرة الحسد التي لم تبرد

translationEn: A caller summoned with the tongue of a guiding, directing voice, and a resolute will answered from a sleeper in his bed. He called out while darkness had spread its curtains and sleep reigned over drowsy eyes. 'O you who drives away the thirst-maddened camels that have gone five days without water, lead them to the watering-troughs of Muhammad.' They stretch their thirsty necks toward the lofty heights of the glory pool. Thoughts awoke and spent the night as anxious whisperings in the heart of a vigil-keeping insomniac. When I saw you, O Muhammad, choosing the purest of praises from the laudation of those who seek, I dispatched my panegyrics into you and left them as white-blazed stars that the transmitters travel by morning and evening. What is it that whenever I train a she-camel for you it comes like a noble she-camel led on a halter? It is nothing but that your flint was not in the hand of its striker as a dull flint. You have validated my praise of you when you looked after me so that I might claim kinship with the witnessed master. I have taken refuge through you with the son of a king whose noble traits have proclaimed his fine lineage. A king who gives generously and does not consult any adviser in it, and the supplicant governs his own favor. He says, with lofty honor surrounding him: 'There is no good in honor if I am not praised.' And I shall be what the seekers of generosity expect of me, and I shall defend my honor with what my hands own. His generosity refuses to let my dignity be ruffled, protecting it with the newly acquired and the inherited. His two palms have two lasting rains for me: an old one of affection and a newer one of gold. How many of your rivals has his right hand stretched out after their ruin into everlasting prosperity! And many a war, he has impregnated and delivered before the natural time of birth. When he dispatches a resolution against oath-breakers, it sweeps their heads off with stationary swords. If the Caliphate were to reward you for a stance you took, it would make your image the qibla of the mosque. Its armies would hasten to you until, when they reached you, every sword-bearer would prostrate before you. God and the Caliph thank a stance of yours widely known at al-Badd, difficult to witness. In a narrow pass, bitter in its cunning, suffocating, crowded from the shattered spears. You fought in it a heretic in his religion — his valor saw you as no heretic. You rose above his skull and its fragments flew off by a death-brand drawn in both hands. O knight of Islam, you have protected it and spared it the treachery of the aggressive enemy. You supported it with battalions you turned into a watchpost targeting the enemy's exposed flanks. You became the key of the frontier-passes and their lock, and the stopper of their breach that was never stopped. You obtained in it the blood of the martyr and his vengeance, and you triumphed in it with the gratitude of every monotheist. The hearts of Mecca laughed for it as they laughed on the day of Badr and the obstinate enemies witnessed it. You revived for Islam the valor of Khalid and widened in it the path for those of Tihama and Najd. If Harthama ibn Ayan were alive in the world and had witnessed his excellence, he would not have denied it. Or had he seen the bitter taste of the fierce war, he would have seen him more crushing to the obstinate rebels. More fleet in running the raiding horses on night marches, and more protective by tongue and by hand. As for the thoroughbreds, they ran and he outpaced them and drank the purest of their spring-water at the watering-place. He left Talha in the dust, and Hatim, and left Aban exhausted, surpassed by his furthest reach. He climbed the stairway of glory until, when he reached the stars, he descended to settle above Fomalhaut. Live in bliss — for the honorific name with which you were named is a good omen that ran for you toward happiness, so be happy! You paid a visit to the Caliph that was proportionate to the stars of greatest fortune. You visited the Caliph with a blessed, remembered visit that cut short the hope of envy. They breathe, and the embers of envy that never cool bend toward their throats. They resented you and sought your bounty but tried to climb a mountain whose flat surface slips under the climber. The plates of their scheming have worn away, as if reminding one of ruins at the dune of Thimhad.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: camels maddened by thirst, or the afflicted with lovesickness
camels that have gone five days without water
a flint that does not spark, dull, inert
frontier-passes, border garrisons
Fomalhaut (a bright star); metaphor for supreme height
oath-breakers, those who violate covenants
al-Badd, the fortress of Babak al-Khurramite in Azerbaijan

context: A grand panegyric to a military commander named Muhammad, widely believed to be Muhammad ibn Humayd al-Tusi who fought against Babak al-Khurramite. Abu Tammam celebrates his martial feats at al-Badd and his visit to the Caliph, comparing his glory to the stars and his enemies' plots to worn ruins.

titleEn: Announce the Death to All the Living

verses.sadr: على خالد بن يزيد بن مز
وقد كان لو رد غرب الحمام
فهل كان مذ كان حتى مضى
مضى خالد بن يزيد بن مز

verses.ajuz: وما قد بنى من جليل البناء

verses.sadr: فقد علم الله أن لن تحب

translationEn: Announce a death announcement to every living person — the youth of the Arabs has taken up residence in the quarter of death. We have all been struck by the arrow of competition; would that we had been struck by the arrow of high price! O Death, you have bereaved us of the water of life and the water of dignity. What have you brought present here, and what have you hidden for those of the tent? Announce, announce the death of the twin brother of generosity — an announcement that profits but little. The two were partners in a single rein, foster-brothers in brotherhood, pure companions. Weep over Khalid ibn Yazid ibn Mazyad with blood-red tears mixed with water. Do not consider weeping a disgrace — it cleaves grief more closely to a burning thirst. For the calamity has multiplied to the measure of tears, and the disaster has grown to the station of weeping. Its interior is a refuge for sorrow and its exterior is a brand of loyalty. Gone is the Wa'ilid king through whom we milked life from a full vessel. So generosity, the fresh-branched, has perished, and youthfulness steeped in prime youth. The heights of glory have bowed their heads for him, and the house of magnanimity has had its tents lowered. He was one of those who illumined the throne and the hall, filling it with splendor. His kingship was recognized from Khalid, as was that of kings — by the crushing of enemies and the banishment of enmity. Was he not the most steadfast of them in combat against lions, and the most given to deer? Did he not drive horses from Babylon, lean like fire-making sticks of the saffron-tree? He extended their whirlwind over the frontier with a sword-sharp stratagem and a spacious spirit. When his banners appeared, blazing like the star of a pre-Islamic sky, and he had blocked the open escape route of the field-mouse and held fast to the secret exit, he folded their affair forcibly in his hands like rolling up a scroll and folding a garment. They submitted — by my life — to the rule of swords, and swords were most worthy of that judgment. Their submission was not to his authority — rather, they submitted to his mastery. We lost the treasure of wealth, while the Imam found himself struck by the treasure of chanted praise. He was not struck in his shepherding of the flock — rather, he was struck in his shepherding of the shepherds. The physician says, when his remedy and medicine were hidden from the disease: the discomfort of his midday rest and his overnight lodging, and the changing airs have slain him swiftly. Yet he would have resisted, had death's force been repelled — powerfully eager for long endurance. His resting-place was in the shadow of swords, and his drink was from the dark blood of veins. The edges of the difficult pulpit were his bed, and the fire of war was his fire for warmth. He had no garment but flowing coats of mail shimmering like the backs of bright water. Was there, from his very beginning until he passed, any praiseworthy sustenance for him other than this? Dhahl ibn Shayban — the Dhahl of pride and the Dhahl of bestowal and the Dhahl of exaltedness — gone. Khalid ibn Yazid ibn Mazyad has departed — the moon of the night, the sun of the forenoon. He has left his deeds among you, so let me not be compared in them with the slow-moving. They received death as men receive it with bitter draught, and wept over him as women weep. My burning thirst for Khalid is Khalid himself, and the guest of my griefs is long in staying. Patience has not shamed me in missing him, nor have I put on the shame of base consolation. I recall the greenness of those times in his presence, and the prosperity of that courtyard. His visitors came for the gifts as if their presence were merely for the gift-giving. And when the knowledge of his assembly was a spring of cool water for those thirsty minds — the calm turning away harm through him, and noblemindedness turning away quarrel. When he was the one who loosened the hobbles of summer and was the key to the lock of winter. My portion from his two palms was not the meager or the insignificant. I saw him with the eye of a leader, and he saw me with the eye of brotherhood. My sighing over Khalid — a sigh that is before me and another behind me. My sighing when he charged into destruction, my sighing when he settled in for the gift-giving. A tomb that has contained the snake of the heretics, and a soft earth that has blocked the way to riches? Go, O king with whom blows the fragrance of the south wind and the scent of rain-clouds — the best of rewards. For how much nobility has the soil hidden, and how much beautiful valor has time devoured! O Abu Jafar, may time rub glory upon you and bring you long life. For your hoped-for rain-cloud is not a barren one, and our wind from you is not the north wind. Nor have those expectations of you returned bewildered, nor has the path of hope been blocked. The frontier has been weakened, so dispatch to it the tips of spears in seeking the cure. For your grandfather's fortune has outpaced the fortune of kings, and your father's life is a recent brilliance. He did not accept the grip of the sword at rest, nor the mantle upon his shoulder for ceremony. He continually ascended those heights alongside the star, wrapped in the primordial cloud. He climbed until even a fool would think he had a dwelling in the sky. We have heard that those wars, when they were sung on with melody, responded with music. And an experienced man kept returning to their relief with the pitch-tar of the camel. He drew bucket after bucket for them like the matching buckets in a pulley, and a bucket when emptied was like other buckets. That forearm was like the twisted strands of its rope, a firm grip on that well-cord. Do not shame his righteous days and what he has built of glorious construction. For God knows that you will never love anything as much as you love the treasure of praise.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: announce a death! a cry proclaiming someone's demise
of the tribe of Wa'il, of noble Bakri Arab lineage
the main burrow entrance of the jerboa (metaphor for an escape route)
the secret exit of the jerboa's burrow (metaphor for a hidden escape)
a skilled physician, a medical expert
full-length coats of mail, complete armor
the north wind, a cold and barren wind

context: A long elegy for Khalid ibn Yazid ibn Mazyad al-Shaybani, the Abbasid governor and military commander. Abu Tammam mourns the patron who was simultaneously a warrior, a dispenser of generosity, and a man of culture, closing with an address to Abu Jafar urging him to take up the frontier defense.

titleEn: He Turned Away, Unexpectedly
translationEn: He turned away — and it was not expected that he would turn away — he did not keep the covenant or the pledge. He did not guard my affection nor my sanctity, though I never ceased guarding affection for him. O you who kills me unjustly with the sword of passion — since I have become a slave, show mercy to the slave! By the One who has tormented my heart with you, I have suffered enormously since you parted from me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to turn away, to rebuff, to estrange oneself
the solemn covenant, the binding pledge
sanctity, inviolable right, honor
I endured, I suffered through
hardship, tremendous effort and suffering

context: A short ghazal lamenting the beloved's sudden estrangement and breach of covenant. The poet invokes God as witness to his suffering and appeals to the beloved's mercy, using the master-slave dynamic common in classical Arabic love poetry.

titleEn: Eyelids of the Supple Willow Branch

verses.sadr: طلعت طلوع الشمس في طرف النوى
فنحرت حسن الصبر تحت الصدر عن
حاشى لجمر حشاي أن يلقى الحشا
فظللت حد الأرض تحت العزم في
تحثو إذا حث العتاق الوخد في
لو فاح عود في الندي وذكره
فإذا بدا في مشهد قامت له
لا يهتدي صرف الزمان إلى امرىء

translationEn: The eyelids of the supple willow-branch are kept from sleep by you. They poured out a stored tear, yellowed, onto a cheek reddened by the rose. It was as if when their thread broke, it was the breaking of a thread of bracelets, necklaces, and clasps. She fanned the intensity of her longing, and the sting of grief set her on fire with a flame that has no extinction. She appeared like the sun rising at the edge of separation, while the sun rises with an envious glance. She contemplated my form with an eye that reinforced the pillars of love in my baptized heart. Then she slaughtered the beauty of patience beneath the breast, away from a neck of bright throat and collar-bone. Far be it from the embers of my innermost being to encounter the innards except with a blast like a fire-blast of kindling. What the fire of my heart has left of me has become a prisoner on the way of the open desert. Shoulders of mounts bearing wealth smile with the shoulders of mounts bearing riding-camels. So I traveled the edge of the earth beneath resolve on a broad-cheeked she-camel that brings near the edge of every far place. She throws dust when the noble swift-trotters stir up the dust in the white blazes of thoroughbreds in a rush of unity. Her resting-place is amid the secrets of her swift-walking, until she knelt down at Ahmad the Praised. I alighted beneath a cloud drenched with the vitality of smiling lightning and thunders. One meets him among the visitors as if he were the moon of the sky glowing among the stars of fortune. If aloeswood were to be fragrant in the assembly and his memory there, the fragrance of his memory would surpass the fragrance of the aloeswood. Al-Mansur entrusted to him the liberality of his right hand, and he passed on — peerlessly unique and yet not lost. He sees the dissipation of wealth as his best treasure, and the eternal memory of praise as the best eternity. Abu al-Hasan displays his gifts and repeats them, so those who hope from him are in a perpetual festival of gifts. I greeted his radiant face with fine, brilliant panegyrics, and he greeted my radiant face with generosity. If he were to aim at a rock-fragment beside a boulder one day, he would crush the side of the boulder. When the frontiers seek his help, the tips of the spears quench their thirst from the frontier-pass and the jugular vein. He draws his resolves after them in the wake of their flight, and covers them with victory and divine support. He has an attentive eye and a keen ear directed toward the hunted, screaming, exhausted man. You encounter him alone and yet think that from his resolve he has with him an army of numbers. O you most-hoped-for king, by whom my intellect sparked the thread of my songs — I am a foot-traveler in the land of Merv, yet mounted in the excellence of poetry over every master of verse. So ennoble the humiliation of my walking with a refined one, sweet in appearance, shaped and well-cut. Of sorrel or roan or bay or jet-black — for his heart's understanding is sound. The glances promenade in his movements as I promenade in your extended shade. Clad in a mantle whose embroidery surpasses in beauty between the processions the embroidery of fine cloaks. When he appears in a gathering, the nobles at the head of the witnessed assembly stand up for him. The rider who goes out upon him finds joy as in the joy of the newborn knight. If the horses race him in their track, the horses throw the key to him. He returns among his stirrup-holders with an opposite bearing, wrapped in the headband of mastery. His escort and his enchanters invoke over him all the protective incantations of praise they know. They fall in love with the fresh glow in his face as a young man falls for the face of a tender young woman. I close the eyelids of my gratitude to you — for it has grown heavy on me because of your existing generosity. I have sought protection in the height of your mountain, for it is a mountain that stands in the stead of an iron mountain. The vicissitudes of time find no way to a man who moves about in your familiar courtyard.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: supple, slender, soft (branch or young woman)
the rosy red coloring, blooming rosiness
a large hard rock, boulder
a strong, broad-cheeked she-camel
the key; also a lead-rope for a horse
one who is hunted, a fugitive, the pursued
Merv, a city in Khorasan (modern Turkmenistan)

context: A qasida to Ahmad, likely a governor in Merv (Khorasan), combining a nasib that describes the beloved woman with a lengthy madih. The poet closes by asking for a horse as a gift to remedy the indignity of traveling on foot. The poem blends the physical description of the beloved with an elaborate description of a fine horse.

titleEn: O Muhammad ibn Said, Reserve Your Grief

verses.sadr: أنت الذي لا تعذل الدنيا إذا
لو كان يغنى حازم عن واعظ
لست الفتى إن لم تعر مدامعا
وإذا رأيت أسى امرئ أو صبره

translationEn: O Muhammad ibn Said, store up your grief in it — keep it behind the hot ember on the day of its thirst. You are the one whom the world does not rebuke when calamities have turned aside from his guilt. Had a wise and resolute man been self-sufficient without a preacher, you would have been self-sufficient through your resolve and your intelligence. You are not truly a man if you do not lend your tears from their water, while passion still has its own water. When you see a man's grief or his patience one day, you have then witnessed the portrait of his inner mind. I see the dust of manliness weeping, and I am almost weeping myself, in awe of its weeping. It is incumbent upon the people of wakefulness and reason, and by the judgment of a physician who knows his own judgment — that a young camel bereaved of its dam not be consoled for its intimate companion until it is first consoled through its own consolation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to store up, to reserve for later
calamities, afflictions that befall
a resolute, firm, strong-willed man
firmness, resolution, self-discipline
manliness, virtue, noble moral qualities
a young camel in its second year; also a young bereaved animal
an intimate friend, a close companion

context: A poem of wisdom and consolation addressed to Muhammad ibn Said, advising him to be patient and to preserve his dignity in grief. Abu Tammam uses the striking image of manliness itself weeping, suggesting that even virtue mourns, and closes with a paradox about the proper order of consolation.

titleEn: I Am in Pain and Severe Grief

verses.sadr: صار ذنبي كذنب آدم يا عمر

translationEn: I am in burning pain and severe grief — there is no addition to my burning for me. By my father as ransom — a fawn from whose eyes I breathed on Thursday the wind of estrangement. My sin has become like the sin of Adam, O passing traveler — and so I was expelled from the gardens of eternity. I would ransom with my life the one with languid eyelids, who is called by a name and whose honorific is part of 'Abd al-Hamid.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: burning pain of grief or love, anguish
a young gazelle, used as a metaphor for the beloved
I breathed in, I sensed (a gentle breeze)
estrangement, rebuffing, turning away
one with languid, still, beautiful eyelids
I would ransom, I would sacrifice myself for

context: A brief ghazal comparing the poet's expulsion from the beloved's favor to Adam's expulsion from paradise. The poem closes with a discreet identification of the beloved by his patronymic — a convention in Abbasid love poetry for referring to male or female beloveds.

titleEn: A Singing Bird Awakened Your Longing

verses.sadr: قد قيل : أين تريد ، قلت : أخا الندى
فالجود حي ما حييت وإن تمت

translationEn: A singing bird aroused your longing when it warbled while the branches swayed. Perch by perch, it called a turtledove, and she called back, sharing passion with him and being lured. Two companions in the shade of the branches united, and between them was entwined a knotted love. They taste each other's saliva, this one feeding that one and that one repeating for this one. O two birds, enjoy yourselves — may you both thrive — and hide it from the morning, for I am exhausted. Ah, the impact of separation, O son of Muhammad — between the lover and the lover it is severe. I weep while lightning has towered, luminous, and thunders from all corners of the sky. The prime of youth stirred, and the villages and open lands lit up at the smile of the trees. The peacocks of Iraq swept by, their gleaming tails shining as they moved swiftly. They strut like virgins circling the shrine-pole as the feast approaches. I shall scatter pearls from my tongue whose thread will return to Iraq, knotted. Until it settles in the station of al-Muhallab among the mansions where glory has been raised. He raised the banner of the Caliphate and men fell short of it, but Dawud seized it. The sayyid of the Atik — uncontested — for there is no sayyid's mastery of another to be found. I knocked by your name in the darkness, dazed: Dawud, indeed in your deeds you are praiseworthy. They asked: 'Where do you intend?' I said: 'I want the brother of generosity and the noble father of Sulayman.' Open the lock of my life with your generosity, for the key to the lock is the existence of your hands for me. Generosity lives as long as you live, and if you die, its springs will dry up and generosity will die.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a melodiously singing bird
a turtledove (also called a moonlit dove)
the prime, the onset of youth
peacocks (plural of طاووس)
a shrine or sacred pole around which people circle
of the Atik clan of Azd, a noble lineage
dazed, confused, overwhelmed

context: A qasida to Dawud ibn al-Muhallab, a governor of the Muhallabid family in Iraq, opening with an unusual bird nasib in which two birds' union contrasts with the poet's own loneliness. Abu Tammam uses the image of peacocks parading through Iraq to set the festive backdrop before praising Dawud's lineage and generosity.

titleAr: هو الدهر لا يشوي وهن المصائب
titleEn: Time Does Not Waste the Weak of Calamities

verses.sadr: هو الدهر لا يشوي وهن المصائب

verses.ajuz: بل الموت لاشك الذي هو غالب

verses.sadr: وقلت أخي ، قالوا أخ ذو قرابة ؟
كأن لم يقل يوما كأن فتنثني

verses.ajuz: وكنت امرءا أبكي دما وهو غائب

verses.sadr: على أنها الأيام قد صرن كلها

translationEn: Such is time — it does not half-burn the weak of calamities, and most of men's hopes are lies. O you who overcome — there is no overcoming of a calamity; rather, death is without doubt the one who overcomes. I said 'my brother,' and they said 'a blood-relation?' I said: 'No, but those of kindred spirit are the closest kin.' My kinsman in resolve, in opinion, and in way — even if pedigree has distanced us in its origins. As if he had never said one day 'as if,' drawing ears toward his speech in eager desire. And as if he had never split the assembly with a decisive word, spear-like, experience engraved on both its sides. And as if I had not drawn off the hidden turmoils of my age through his counsel — so that his counsel and my calamities never coincided. My companion has passed, leaving behind grief and sorrow — and I was a man who wept blood even while he was merely absent. Yet such are the days — they have become entirely wonders, until there is no wonder in them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to half-roast, to half-do; here: does not take the weak as its aim
those of similar nature, kindred souls
the assembly, the gathering place
decisive, cutting (word or judgment)
to pick up (gradually), to draw off, to glean
grief, anguish secretly harbored
sorrow, deep sadness

context: An elegy for a close companion defined not by blood-kinship but by intellectual and spiritual affinity — a theme rare in classical Arabic poetry. Abu Tammam mournfully celebrates a friend whose eloquence split assemblies, and ends with the wry paradox that days become so full of wonders that wonders lose their wonder.

titleEn: One Who Enchants with His Glance and Cheek

verses.sadr: واحمر حتى كدت أن لا أرى

translationEn: An enchanter of glances and cheeks, of proportionate stature and figure — his beauty has made me his slave, while my gaze has made him mine. He spoke, while my eye was in his eye, grazing in the garden of eternity. 'Your eye has adorned you,' I said — 'my tears by its patience are more than measure.' And it reddened until I could barely see his cheek from the abundance of the rose-color. Beauty and fragrance, when they combine, are two slaves to me — for Abu Abd.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: an enchanter, one who tempts and captivates
sidelong glances, the corners of the eyes
grazing freely, roaming in luxury
the garden of eternity, paradise
roses; also the rosy color of a cheek
Abu Abd — a kunya (honorific) used to identify the beloved

context: A playful ghazal on mutual enchantment: the beloved has enslaved the poet through beauty, while the poet's gaze has captivated the beloved in turn. The poem ends with a paradox that beauty and fragrance together become slaves to 'Abu Abd,' the beloved's honorific.

titleEn: The Prince Has Taken the Place of Every Helper

verses.ajuz: في العالمين وكم له من حامد
إذ ليس جدي في الجدود بصاعد

verses.sadr: ما لي حرمت لديك حظوة خالد

translationEn: The prince has taken the place of the helper's support and of the one who freely grants his acquired and inherited wealth. God bless you — what a noble, glorious man, easy in his nature, singular in acts of generosity! Time is generous with that which bestows wealth upon one who hopes, whether departing or arriving. So why have I become excluded from your bounty, while others are looked upon by you with a father's eye? How many a grateful man does Prince Muhammad have in all the worlds, and how many a one who praises him! Despair has kept me in the place of the seated one, since my fortune among fortunes does not rise. Why have I been deprived with you of Khalid's favor? Am I not the sanctity from Khalid? The scarcity of men has raised the value of Khalid's favor, and the summer has made the market of the cold one's goods boom. Two liars whose speech is obscenity have settled at your court in the place of Amr the ascetic!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: support, assistance, a gift given in support
the helper, the supporter
newly acquired wealth
inherited, ancient wealth
the glorious, the noble of deed
a liar, a fabricator
obscenity, vile speech

context: A complaint-poem (shikwa) addressed to Prince Muhammad, lamenting that the poet has been excluded from patronage while unworthy flatterers have taken his place. Abu Tammam invokes the name of Khalid (a previous patron) to strengthen his claim and criticizes the two liars who have replaced the virtuous Amr at the prince's court.

titleEn: A Deaf Fortune Heedless of Reproach

verses.sadr: جف در الدنيا فقد أصبحت تك
إن ريب الزمان يحسن أن يه
لم تدر عينه عن الحمس حتى
يا شهابا خبا لآل عبيد الل

translationEn: The misfortune of time is deaf to reproach, lying in wait with fears and afflictions. The udder of the world has dried — it has come to consume our souls without reckoning. Had it appeared unveiled it would have been despised, but people's hearts became attached to its beauty in the veil. The calamities of time excel at directing disasters toward those of noble lineage. For this reason, after turning green, the meadow of the highlands dries before the meadow of the lowlands. Its eye did not stop at the resolute until it shook the corner-stone of Himyar's lords. It seized from them the pearl of the deep — how beautiful — and the idol of the prayer-niche. The purest of the pure and the most splendid of the splendid, and the very essence of essence. O Muhammad, the radiant one has gone from your clear days — what a departure! The tomb and the earth grimaced from you with a face not really frowning nor scowling. The tomb and the earth extinguished your glowing heart-torch in a time of mental darkness. You exchanged for a dwelling manifest in seriousness, called the severer of all connections. A lonely dwelling, even though it swarms with the settlement of friends and beloved ones. O shooting-star that has been extinguished for the family of Ubaydallah — how grievous is the loss of this shooting-star! A fresh blossom from which glory opened in a grove of beautiful surroundings. A character like wine or like the saliva of musk, or like perfume, or like aloe-water. A greeting that suffices without any inadequacy, and a morning radiance illuminated without affliction. The days brought him down from their back after his foot had been firmly in the stirrup — just when youth soared and the world's doors were open to him. He resembled the adorned sharp sword, save that his adornment was the jewels of culture. He had fresh opinions and bold resolve, then the freshness of giving, the freshness of youth. Death set its course toward him until it granted his beautiful face to the soil.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fears, terrors, anxieties
pains, illnesses, afflictions
the resolute, the hard-hearted, those of firm determination
the saliva of musk, a metaphor for fine fragrance
a type of fine perfume, saffron-tinged
the adorned sharp sword (a simile for a cultured young man)
the idol or painted figure of the prayer-niche, a standard of beauty

context: An elegy for a young man named Muhammad from the family of Ubaydallah, cut down in the prime of youth. Abu Tammam meditates on the unfairness of time, which targets the most noble, and lingers over the beauty and promise of the deceased in images of freshness — wine, musk, dawn light — before surrendering him to the earth.

titleAr: رأيت في النوم أن الصلح قد فسدا
titleEn: I Saw in a Dream the Peace Was Broken

verses.sadr: رأيت في النوم أن الصلح قد فسدا
verses.ajuz: أن مولاي بعد القرب قد بعدا

verses.sadr: لم لم أمت حزنا لم لم أمت أسفا
verses.ajuz: لم لم أمت جزعا لم لم أمت كمدا

verses.sadr: قد كدت احلف لولا أن ذا سرف
أصبحت من زفرات لا أقوم بها

translationEn: I saw in a dream that the peace had been broken — that my master, after closeness, had grown distant. Why did I not die of grief, why did I not die of regret, why did I not die of anguish, why did I not die of heartbreak! I nearly swore — were it not excess — that I would never taste sleep again after it. I have woken from sighs I cannot bear, complaining of sleep while others complain of sleeplessness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: reconciliation, peace, truce
my master, my lord (also used for a beloved)
from suppressed grief, hidden heartbreak
excess, going beyond proper bounds
deep sighs, heavy exhalations of grief
sleeplessness, wakefulness from worry

context: A brief ghazal lamenting a bad dream about estrangement from the beloved. The anaphoric repetition of 'why did I not die' and the final paradox — complaining of sleep while others suffer sleeplessness — are characteristic of Abu Tammam's rhetorical wit.

titleEn: Nawar Among Her Companions

verses.sadr: إذا ما شعر قوم كان ليلا

translationEn: Nawar among her companions is Nawar — as if she confronted you as a flock or a herd. A liar's slander has been believed and hearts have distanced themselves in obedience to a slanderer, and abodes have grown far. Stop, let us give the dwellings drink from eyes that have deep watering-pools of longing. Their traces have been obliterated — and what dwelling can maintain its choice state against time? Hearthstones like cheeks slapping themselves in grief, and a trench-ring like a broken bracelet. There was a burning pain, then it settled — for every questioner there is a resting-place. The kings have passed and been extinguished, and the nobles of our rulers have become mere traders. Standing in the shadows of blame, protecting their dirhams while not protecting honor. If only the slumbers of time were lifted from him, and the covering stripped from his shoulders — he would distribute provisions fairly among us. But our time is a donkey! The riding-camels and their riders will be roused by a young man like a sword whose sleep is its edge. He has overlooked both horizons until the earth looks to him like a single yard. The envious say when we turn away: 'They have cut a road or raided.' We head toward Abu al-Husayn — and once he was a youth whose appointments' lives were short. He has a trait that the Quran has forbidden — that being his lavish, immediate generosity. No harm came from you, but the seas persisted in their nature. Through his generosity the fruit of hopes becomes sweet, and the burning ambitions are quenched at his side. I have raised the full-formed verses of poetry in his honor as a lighthouse is raised for those who look. Forbearing he is — and restraint is a tent-peg of his — and what fire has no sparks? His enemies yearn for him after their demands are satisfied, and bring forth (benefit) as ten-month-pregnant camels bring forth. I see two wine-vessels in your presence, aloof, yet each one is pure gold. When a people's poetry is darkness, they burst open like the dawn breaking. And if their odes are barren, they take on color like the camomile doubled in bloom. I have adorned them and others with the embellishment of your generosity — and rhymes do grow jealous! Another before you puts on kindness as a disguise and takes from his promises nothing but emptiness. I saw good deeds that were twisted and turned into sacrificial animals, with procrastination as their slaughter-knives. Procrastination in beginning a promised deed was smoke for the beneficence that was the fire. Stinginess and procrastination have been kin since both existed — if not by lineage, then by proximity. For this it is said: some withholding is nearer to nobility, and some giving is shameful. Leave the mention of estates — I feel restless when they are mentioned, and I feel an aversion to them. My only estate is the riding-camels and poetry that is neither sold nor lent. And what am I to do with real property — I have no security in it — but your generosity is my real property.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a woman's name, meaning 'white flower' or 'blooms'
a herd of wild cows or deer
hearthstones (tripod stones placed around a fire)
a trench dug around a tent to divert rainwater
ten-month-pregnant camels, close to giving birth
procrastination in fulfilling a promise
estates, agricultural holdings, landed property

context: A long qasida to Abu al-Husayn, opening with a nasib about Nawar and a critique of the age's degenerate kings before pivoting to praise. Abu Tammam includes a remarkable digression on the corruption of gift-giving culture — where procrastination slaughters beneficence — and concludes by declaring that his only wealth is poetry and riding-camels.

titleEn: What Generosity Between the Earth and Grain
translationEn: What generosity between the moistened earth and the grain — what pliable mastery and sound judgment! O son of Abu Rabi, the world has received your day with a terrible day. It has torn the collars of men who, had they been able, would have torn what lies beneath those collars. You were near to me in absence and have now, in nearness, become no longer near. The delegations of the earth returned from his tomb with empty hands and full hearts. You know well what loss you have suffered — the loss of the sun is only known after sunset. When the far-dwelling one used to visit him, he would alight at a fertile pond and valley. The hands of the white camels brought him near to a courtyard that was like the birthplace of a stranger. After him, hopes have darkened and been stripped of every beauty and sweetness. There were cheeks polished for a time — today they have become the abode of pallor. How many a need became ride-able through him that before him was not fit to ride! He untied its hobbles as the south wind releases from the cloud's cord. When we aimed toward him in seeking, he was a well or the well-rope of the well. And a favor from him that I wore as a garment — it was like the hem of a brand-new robe. Of those blessings that, if a grateful man grows slack, stand in for their benefactor in the role of the preacher. When they stop, they travel on displaying his excellence; or if he is absent one day, they make him present by the absent.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: generosity; also moisture, dew
pliable, flexible mastery — greatness that bends without breaking
a sound, firm, cruciform (decisive) judgment
loud weeping, sobbing
a well, especially an unlined, ancient well
brand-new, freshly made (garment)
pallor, the fading of color from the face

context: An elegy for ibn Abi Rabi, a generous patron who is mourned as the sun — his loss only truly felt after his setting. Abu Tammam expresses the paradox that delegations left his tomb empty-handed yet with full hearts, and describes how the patron's blessings themselves continue to speak on his behalf even after his death.

titleEn: You Have Brought Me Beyond the Peak of Grief
translationEn: You have brought me to beyond the peak of suppressed grief — you have made my eye weep until the end of eternity. O my liver! The watcher is about to prevent me from even saying 'O my liver!' I do not blame the envious ones, O most beautiful of people, for their unanimous envy of me. How can I blame the envious for envying you, when they have seen the crescent moon of the sky at my command?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: suppressed grief, hidden heartbreak
the watcher, the spy, the guardian preventing lovers' meeting
O my liver! — an exclamation of grief and longing
the envious one
the crescent moon — a simile for a beautiful beloved

context: A witty four-verse ghazal that ends with a clever turn: the poet says he cannot blame the envious, because even he himself, at whose 'command' the crescent moon-like beloved stands, can understand why they are envious. The 'crescent moon' is a classical Arabic metaphor for a beautiful face.

titleEn: Tell the Generous Prince

verses.sadr: لي صاحب قد كان لي مؤنسا

translationEn: Tell the generous, open-handed prince, whose two palms suffice for the Bedouin and the townsman — may the days recompense you with ample room and freshness from my fresh branch! I thank a blessing from you that is worth thanking — and one who is ungrateful for blessings is like an unbeliever. A gift that could only be for one whose rank is situated in an abundant position. May you never cease to be, from my gratitude, in a garment worn by one with spoils of glory. He says, one whose ears are struck: 'How much did the first leave for the last?' I have a companion who used to be my intimate and my familiar in the time gone by. He milks the turns of fortune and mixes the sweet with the sour. Until, when my meadow sang with his flies in a beautiful, blooming spring, he impregnated its desires with resolve after it had embraced a barren ambition. The riding-camels carry from him a wonder that renews the mockery for the mocker — a man of wealth seeking from a petitioner, and a silenced one taking from a poet! I found my wealth, through his approach, a wish from a stumbling hope. Let the loser share in it with you — and do not be the partner of the gambling man. For your gift to the visitor is glory, and nothing like your gift to a visitor equaling a visitor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the open-handed, the liberal, the one whose spirit is free
ample room, breadth, spaciousness
the repeated milkings between two successive milkings (the turns of fate)
sour milk, curdled milk; bitter experience
barren (of a woman or an ambition)
the one who has lost at gambling
the gambler, the one who gambles

context: A panegyric of thanks to a generous prince, interspersed with reflections on a companion's unexpected behavior — a man of wealth who begged from a petitioner and a poet. Abu Tammam uses this digression to heighten the contrast with the patron's own magnanimity.

titleEn: Weeping Is My Eye's Habit, Grief Is Mine

verses.ajuz: فاتركيني وقيت ما بي لما بي

verses.sadr: فيك يا أحمد بن هارون خصت

translationEn: The habit of my eye is weeping, and grief is my habit — so leave me, may you be protected, to what afflicts me, in what afflicts me. I shall repay the remaining days of my life amid my secret grief, my tears, and my depression. In you, O Ahmad ibn Harun, my calamity has been specific, then spread to all. The days have bereaved me of you, so my consolation lies in my disarray and my restraint in my disturbance. The days have bereaved me of the truthful one of eloquent speech — the youth of noble deeds and culture. Of a friend beyond all friends — no, rather, my companion chosen above all my companions. A man of Shaymar's tenacity, who occupies in my lineage stations of the most noble and the pure-bred. No sooner had he clothed himself in glory and donned the mantle of praise in the finest manner — when the eyes of those who gazed upon him saw him as a brilliant moon and a lion of the jungle — and the water of flowing generosity rose on his cheeks, and the water of wisdom, and the water of youth — than Death dispatched toward him a piercing eye that severed from him the strongest of bonds.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: habit, continuous practice, nature
secret grief, internal anguish
depression, despondency
calamity of loss, grievous bereavement
a lion, especially a courageous lion
his two cheeks, the sides of his face
the pure-bred, the pure in lineage

context: An elegy for Ahmad ibn Harun, a close companion and friend of Abu Tammam. The poem is deeply personal — Abu Tammam calls him not merely a friend but his chosen companion above all others — and mourns the paradox that death struck precisely at the moment of his full flowering in glory, wisdom, and youth.

titleAr: وفى البكا بالعهد اذ لم يكن
titleEn: Weeping Kept the Covenant

verses.sadr: وفى البكا بالعهد اذ لم يكن

translationEn: Weeping kept the covenant when patience had neither pledge nor covenant. You have spoiled the beauty of the fresh narcissus since the narcissus and the rose come together.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: kept the covenant, fulfilled the promise
a solemn covenant, a binding pledge
the narcissus flower; also a metaphor for beautiful eyes
the rose; also the rosy blush of the cheek
you have soured, you have spoiled the pleasure of

context: A compact two-verse fragment (miqta) in which weeping takes the role of a faithful companion when patience has abandoned the field. The second verse layers imagery: 'narcissus and rose' together usually mean beauty, but here they become something painful, as the beloved's presence is now bittersweet.

titleEn: Muhammad, I Would Be Blameworthy Without You

verses.ajuz: إذا ما لساني خانني فيك أوشكري

verses.sadr: لئن بقيت لي فيك آثار منطق
verses.ajuz: لقد بقيت ثار كفيك في دهري
لأمر العلى فاخترت شكري على عذري
بدائعها ما استحسن الناس من شعري
وذكرتني ما قد نسيت من الشكر

translationEn: Muhammad — I would indeed be blameworthy after all of this if my tongue betrayed me in praising you or my gratitude failed. If there remain in me traces of speech about you, then the traces of your hands' bounty remain in my life. You have met the reversals of fate on my behalf, following the command of excellence, and I have chosen my gratitude over my excuses. You have favored me in calamities with benefactions as if their hands had been opened from the sea. Noble qualities that, if they had been part of my poetry, would have made the wonders of it outshine all that people have deemed beautiful in my verse. You have taught me to clothe praise upon its worthy recipients, and reminded me of the gratitude I had forgotten.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: blameworthy, reviled
the reversals of fate, the vicissitudes of time
calamities, misfortunes that befall
benefactions, crafted deeds of generosity
its hands, its benefits (metaphor for the reach of generosity)
wonders, exquisite novelties

context: A short but dense poem of gratitude to Muhammad, in which Abu Tammam reflects that the patron's qualities are so extraordinary that if they were expressed as poetry they would surpass all his own verse. The closing verse — 'you reminded me of gratitude I had forgotten' — is a graceful compliment suggesting the patron embodies virtue itself.

titleAr: جفوف البلى أسرعت في الغصن الرطب
titleEn: The Drying of Decay Hastened Upon the Wet Branch

verses.sadr: جفوف البلى أسرعت في الغصن الرطب
verses.ajuz: وخطب الردى والموت أبرحت من خطب

verses.sadr: لقد شرقت في الشرق بالموت غادة
verses.ajuz: تعوضت منها غربة الدار في الغرب
هلال عليه نسج ثوب من الترب
من الكرب روح الموت شر من الكرب

verses.sadr: لقد نزلت ضنكا من اللحد والثرى
verses.ajuz: ولو كان رحب الذرع ماكان بالرحب
فقد نقلت بعدي عن البعد والقرب

translationEn: The drying of decay has hastened upon the wet branch, and the calamity of ruin and death has brought the severest of calamities. In the East, a woman choked on death, and I found my compensation in the exile of her dwelling in the West. He clothed me in a garment of grief and sorrow — a crescent whose garment was then woven by the soil. I say, when they say she has found rest in her death from distress: the relief of death is worse than the distress. She has descended into the narrowness of the tomb and the earth — and even were it of wide expanse, it would not be spacious. I used to hope for nearness while she was distant — and now she has been moved beyond nearness and distance. She has a dwelling beneath the earth, yet I have long known her to have a dwelling between the ribs and the heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: decay, wearing away, the process of deterioration
choked on, was afflicted in the East
anguish, choking distress
narrowness, constriction
the ribs, the chest-cavity
a crescent moon (also a person's name here)

context: An elegy for a woman — likely a relative — who died in the East while the poet was in the West. Abu Tammam rejects the conventional consolation that death is a relief, insisting instead that her tomb is a narrow prison, while closing with the touching statement that her true dwelling was always within his chest.

titleEn: Separation Has Snatched Ahmad ibn Yazid
translationEn: Separation has snatched Ahmad ibn Yazid — the deeds of the days are not praiseworthy. Estrangement has distanced one whom I shall not name, and today I am in the near-yet-far. Two separations have struck me: one from a separation and another from a rebuffing. He who was merely absent and missed by the eye is not like the one who is present yet lost.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to snatch away, to steal by stealth
separation, parting, distance
estrangement, abandonment, cutting off
rebuffing, turning away, rejection
the one who is present yet lost — someone there in body but gone in spirit

context: A four-verse poem lamenting two simultaneous losses: Ahmad ibn Yazid who has departed, and an unnamed person (a beloved) who has turned away. The final verse — distinguishing the physically absent from the 'present-yet-lost' — is a celebrated example of Abu Tammam's philosophical concision.

titleAr: لا أنت أنت ولا الديار ديار
titleEn: You Are No Longer You, the Abodes No Longer Abodes

verses.sadr: لا أنت أنت ولا الديار ديار
إذ لا صدوف ولا كنود اسماهما
في حيث يمتهن الحديث لذي الصبا
إذ في القتادة وهي أبخل أيكة

verses.ajuz: إذ لاح أن الصدق منه نهار

verses.sadr: لولا جلاد أبي سعيد لم يزل
حتى التوى من نقع قسطلها على
إلا تكن حصرت فقد أضحى لها
لو طاوعتك الخيل لم تقفل بها

verses.ajuz: أو تثن عنه البيض وهي حرار

verses.sadr: إلا تفر فقد أقمت وقد رأت
في حيث تستمع الهرير إذا علا
فمضى لو أن النار دونك خاضها
أن لست نعم الجار للسنن الأولى
verses.ajuz: إلا إذا ما كنت بئس الجار

verses.sadr: لا يأسفون إذا هم سمنت لهم
والبيض تعلم أن دينا لم يضع
ومودتي لك لا تعار بلى إذا
ولذاك شعري فيك قد سمعوا به

translationEn: You are no longer you, and the abodes are no longer abodes — passion has lightened and desires have departed. Dwelling near the ruins and their people was once for a time the sweetness of a watering-place — now they are seas. Days when those idols would make his eye bleed within them, and the moons would madden his mind. When there was no one named Suduf or Kanud as with the two meant, nor was Nawar Nawar. Fair-skinned ones — when they glance unveiled they are images; when they are looked at, they are herds. Where speech is made plain for the young man, and secrets and hidden things are guarded. When even in the thorny qatad, the most miserly of thickets, there was fruit — and when the branch of time was still pure gold. The news has become clear from its pure substance, and the cities have rejoiced in your conquests. A report whose light has cleared the rust from hearts, when it became clear that its truth is daylight. Without the sword of Abu Said, the frontier's chest would never cease being exposed without a garment. You drove the thoroughbreds as if they were eagles in the villages of Derulia with their nests. Until the whirlwind of their dust-column twisted over the walls of Constantinople. You kindled a fire before the Bosphorus for its people, whose sparks are behind the strait. Even if you did not besiege it, it has come to endure from the fear of a sudden assault something like a siege. Had the horses obeyed you completely, you would not have returned — and the lock has sharpness and no nail. When they met you, they leaned on one another and made excuses in flight, but their excuses availed them nothing. Here is the fire of battle blazing, and here is an army with a clamor, and there is a raid. They were humbled by your assault, which is in their eyes like death that comes with no disgrace. When you issued from the mountain-passes toward them with a vast army that shook the earth. If he sets out at dawn, the landmarks of the road-markers guide him; if he travels by night, the stars are his lighthouse. The White Baths are their appointment, the Fortress is the decisive end, and the Strait is their password. They knew that their raiding was like his raiding of them, and that his raiding of them means destruction. So walking is a whisper and calling is a gesture — from fear of your retribution — and conversation is a secret. Only if the tips of spears do not reach Manuyil or the white blades do not turn him back while still hot. For he has wished that every city were a deaf mountain and every fortress a cave. Unless you flee — but you have stayed, and your eyes have seen the measure of war and how it is avoided. Where you hear the roaring when it rises high, and see the dust of death when it is stirred. Look with a brave eye — and you will know that the standing-ground is where the flight is. When their remnants came to you, you reinforced them with the foremost of tears, and they were many. You gave the examples of the humiliated, though you see that other than that is the deficiency and the bitterness. Patience is more beautiful, and fate is imposed — so accept it, for in its evil there is a choice. Far from it — a courageous man pulled at the reins against you, giving the spear-points all they choose. He went on — had fire been before him he would have waded through it with the sword, unless it were the fire. Until the right returns and it is the one that avenges itself upon you, and Islam has no blood-vengeance among you. God bless Abu Said — he is pure to the guest with no adulteration in him. When you settled at the frontier, it became high, and for the Byzantines that neighborhood was a neighborhood. They became certain, when your sea surged and that roar rose high and that sting became mighty, that you were not a good neighbor to the old ways unless you were a terrible neighbor. Vigilant — the polytheists fear his fervor; humble — the mighty submit to him. He pacifies his mounts when his journeys are delayed, and his concerns are journeys. He travels by night when worries travel, as if he were the star of darkness, and he raids when raids are made. His lineage soared through him among a people for whom the pivot of battle is set and revolving. They do not regret that their honors have grown fat while their lives grow lean. He plants in his planting ground his supporters — at the combat, they are as if they were the Ansar. He has discarded the merchants' character, though they too, by what they have stored for him, will indeed be merchants. Veteran men whom he has given to drink from his valor — yet when they meet the foe, they are as if inexperienced. He returns with joy to the spear-thrust — his encounter is a danger when the quivering spear quivers. The white swords know that no faith has been lost since they were drawn, and no honor has been more neglected. When the curved bows send their arrows flying like locusts spreading when disturbed — their shafts have guaranteed and their strings have pledged that they will snap the strings of the enemy. So leave the path, O sons of the path, to one who knows how the great army is led. Even if your long arms fell short of him, how can they reach when they are short? He is the star of Islam — whatever darkness he pierces, the core of unbelief within it turns hollow. You left their land with your cavalry in the heat of battle until even its most fortified parts were a race-track for them. You settled there calmly and at ease, until we thought it was your own home. The king is pleased with you for it, its bones are mended, and the world has stability in you. I see the meadows pregnant and giving birth since you were among them, and the clouds are ten-month camels. Our days are polished at their edges by you, and the nights are all pre-dawns. Your suppliants are moistened for the suppliants, and the visitors come gently to your visitors. My ambitions are suspended by you, their necks in chains — for loyalty is a bond. My love for you cannot be borrowed — unless it is the blood of the heart that can be lent. As for other people — my sitting-garment has not changed at their departure, whether they have gone to Najd or Tihama. And for that, my poetry about you — they have heard it as enchantment, while my poetry about them is mere verse. Live in safety — may destruction not cease to favor you among us, and may fate fall short of you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: desires, needs, things longed for
Constantinople (modern Istanbul), the Byzantine capital
the Bosphorus strait
Manuel, a Byzantine military commander
the Helpers of Medina who aided the Prophet
the blood of the heart, the innermost part of the heart
those who seek generosity, petitioners

context: One of Abu Tammam's grandest qasidas, addressed to Abu Said, a commander who led a campaign against Constantinople, reaching the walls of the Byzantine capital. The poem opens with a nasib recalling the beauty of youth and beloved women, then transitions into one of the most vivid battle descriptions in classical Arabic poetry, celebrating the terror the commander struck into the Byzantines.

titleEn: Repeat the Lamentation, O Wailing Woman
translationEn: Repeat the lamentation, O wailing woman, repeat — and add to your weeping, then add! Rise unveiled among the unveiled women, baring throats and cheeks. This is the calamity that has invented disasters and said to the eyes of both mankind and jinn: weep freely! Alas — Khorasan has been bereaved of its youth on the morning Umayr ibn al-Walid was laid to rest. Alas — bereaved of an answering, generous one! Alas — bereaved of a lavish bestower! Know that generosity and giving have settled where you have settled in the depths of the plain. My life be your ransom — a king whom his fate struck with an arrow of true destruction. The thick of battle was lifted from him with his face stained by his copious blood. O sea of death, you have taken from him the sea of generosity in a year of hardness. O lion of death, you have mauled from him — on the morning you mauled him — the lion of lions. Did you indeed maul the valiant hero from him? Yes, and also the slayer of the valiant hero. He faced the spears while the faces of death showed themselves in red and black. The veiled man among them had no head veiled — save that he had veiled himself in iron. What a battle! A grand calamity that lent grief and yearning to the patience of the patient man. What a battlefield! It gifted a burning thirst to our livers for all eternity. Yet our prince spared nothing of counsel and justice for the subjects and the soldiers. He poured the gift of his palms upon them and was generous with the newly acquired and the inherited. He faced death in their stead in the open until death gave him to drink from a pool of colocynth. They did not triumph over him until they received as their guest black vulture-eagles and desert hyenas — with a stabbing into their throats, persistent, and a striking on their heads, relentless. O Tuesday, we drank your morning as a terrible morning in its arrival. O Tuesday, we have been struck in you by the loss of the sturdy support. How many eyes of ours have you heated, and how many fortunes of ours have you made stumble! Your birds gave no omen of good crossing, and your stars did not rise auspiciously. O king clothed in the garment of death in a dug-out grave — I came to the courtyard of your gate and was seized by a grief between the throat and the jugular vein. I saw in it riderless mounts and horses standing at the threshold. They were equipment meant either for freeing a captive or for killing a stubborn tyrant. I saw your hopeful ones — upon them there have descended visitations that led them up difficult slopes. And the fortunes that were rising with you have now sunk at the side of others. All of them have prepared provisions and set a station at your side, and packed a riding-camel for a sitting-passenger. The delegations to you have come to a standstill — no benefit remains for one who seeks benefit. Indeed the eyes of generosity have been scalded at what has been intended, and the brilliant foreheads of poetry have been struck down.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a wailing woman lamenting loudly
Khorasan, the northeastern province of the Abbasid caliphate
one thrown down, felled by destruction
large black vultures, carrion birds
colocynth, a bitter desert plant; a metaphor for a lethal, bitter drink
the threshold, the door-sill of a tent or house
the two weights — mankind and jinn together

context: A long elegy for Umayr ibn al-Walid, a military commander in Khorasan. Abu Tammam mourns him as the sea and lion of generosity, curses the Tuesday on which he died, and vividly describes arriving at the abandoned courtyard where the patron's mounts stand riderless. It is one of his most dramatic elegies.

titleEn: I Shall Never Eat Apples
translationEn: I shall never eat apples all my life — even if they were plucked for me from the gardens of eternity. By God, I am not leaving them from aversion — rather, I am leaving them for the cheeks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: apples; also a metaphor for rosy, apple-like cheeks
I harvested it, I plucked it
the gardens of eternity, paradise
aversion, hatred, dislike
cheeks; here the beloved's apple-like cheeks

context: A witty two-verse epigram exploiting the resemblance between apples and rosy cheeks: the poet swears off apples not from dislike but from devotion — he saves them (metaphorically) for the beloved's cheeks. A classic example of Abu Tammam's playful wit in the shorter lyric form.
